name: New Arrivals
description: The latest work from our virtual artists — freshly published and available for the first time. From Vera Voss's gilded figuratives to Elara Chen's graphic provocations, this is the gallery at its most current.

name: Natural Forms
description: Works rooted in the organic world — botanical surrealism, dreamlit forests, and figures dissolving into nature. River Strand and Nova Ash share a reverence for the living world that makes this collection feel like a deep breath.

name: Midnight Abstractions
description: Works that live in shadow and candlelight — Vera Voss's burnished figuratives and Ren Cross's Victorian uncanny. These pieces reward sustained attention, revealing new depths the longer you sit with them.

name: First Light
description: Pieces infused with warmth, wonder, and the quality of morning — Nova Ash's dreamscapes and Vera Voss's most luminous compositions. This collection is for spaces that deserve gentleness.

name: Urban Fragments
description: Elara Chen's complete gallery — high-contrast graphic canvases that carry the visual authority of political art and the emotional depth of portraiture. Work that demands your attention and rewards your consideration.

name: Wunderkammer
description: A cabinet of beautiful curiosities — Mae Ryden's pop surrealist tableaux and Fenna Haas's creature portraits, each piece asking more questions than it answers. For collectors who like their walls to think back at them.

name: Eastern Traditions
description: Hiro Tomo's irezumi-inspired canvases and Elara Nakamura's woodblock-meets-Art-Nouveau panels — two artists working in dialogue with Japanese tradition and arriving at something entirely their own.

name: Old Masters, New Tricks
description: Rave Zola's classical figurative works deploy Old Master technique as a Trojan horse — the Caravaggio lighting, the Velázquez composition, the Baroque drama — all carrying subjects that could not be more of this moment.

name: Small Wonders
description: Bea Gomez's graphic illustrations of everyday objects — the coffee cup, the dog in the doorway, the reading pile — and Fenna Haas's small-format creature portraits. Work that finds the monumental in the unremarkable.
